text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

EXAMINATION: Computed tomography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old male with 3-week history of gross hematuria, right flank pain, and 15-pound unintentional weight loss. Evaluate for renal mass and extent of disease.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 120 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast at a rate of 4 mL/sec. Axial images were obtained during corticomedullary and nephrographic phases. Coronal and sagittal reformats were generated. No prior imaging available for comparison.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: There are multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules of varying size, distributed in both lungs, without a dominant central or endobronchial component. The largest discrete nodule is located in the posterior basal segment of the right lower lobe, measuring approximately 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension, and demonstrates soft tissue attenuation without calcification. An additional nodule in the left lower lobe measures approximately 0.8 cm. A smaller nodule in the right upper lobe apical-posterior segment measures approximately 0.6 cm. No spiculated margins, pleural tags, or satellite nodules to suggest primary lung malignancy. The distribution pattern and size spectrum of these nodules are most consistent with hematogenous metastatic deposits given the clinical context. No cavitation. No endobronchial lesions. The central airways are patent. No lobar or segmental atelectasis. No consolidation to suggest pneumonia.

Pleura and Pericardium: No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pericardial effusion.

Mediastinum and Hila: The heart is normal in size. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysm or dissection. Mediastinal soft tissues are unremarkable. No enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes by size criteria. No superior vena cava obstruction.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No chest wall masses or osseous destructive lesions. No axillary lymphadenopathy.

ABDOMEN:

Right Kidney and Primary Mass: There is a very large, heterogeneous, predominantly solid mass arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney, measuring approximately 11.4 cm craniocaudally x 9.8 cm transversely x 10.1 cm in the anteroposterior dimension. The mass exhibits marked internal heterogeneity with irregular areas of low attenuation centrally, consistent with extensive necrosis and/or cystic degeneration. Peripheral nodular and septal enhancement is present on the nephrographic phase, with areas of more avid arterial-phase enhancement suggesting a hypervascular component. The mass distorts and replaces the upper and mid pole of the right kidney, with a small residual functioning lower pole cortex identified. The interface between the mass and the perirenal fat is locally irregular, with stranding of the perinephric fat, suggesting fat plane encroachment, although definitive Gerota fascia invasion is not clearly established on this study. The mass makes close contact with and appears to invade the right renal vein, which is expanded and contains heterogeneous soft tissue filling its lumen extending to the level of the renal vein-IVC junction. The inferior vena cava at the level of the right renal vein ostium appears patent on the available images; however, given the proximity of the thrombus to the IVC, dedicated MRI of the abdomen is recommended for definitive assessment of IVC involvement prior to surgical planning. There is no identifiable involvement of the left renal vein or IVC above the renal hilum on this examination.

Left Kidney: The left kidney is normal in size and morphology with a smooth cortical surface and uniform cortical enhancement. No mass, stone, or hydronephrosis. The left renal vein and artery appear patent.

Right Adrenal Gland: The right adrenal gland is difficult to confidently separate from the superior aspect of the mass on axial imaging; however, in the coronal reformats the gland is visible as a distinct structure superior to the mass and does not appear to be directly invaded. Dedicated MRI will provide better characterization.

Left Adrenal Gland: Normal appearance.

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth margins and homogeneous parenchyma. No focal hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Spleen: Normal size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: Normal morphology and enhancement without ductal dilatation or peripancreatic stranding.

Gallbladder and Biliary Tree: The gallbladder is present and contains no stones. No pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Stomach and Bowel: No gastric or bowel wall thickening to suggest mass or ischemia. No obstruction. Appendix is visualized and unremarkable.

Retroperitoneal Lymph Nodes: There is prominent retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. A right para-aortic lymph node is enlarged, measuring approximately 2.8 cm in short axis, with mildly heterogeneous internal architecture. An aortocaval lymph node measures approximately 1.9 cm in short axis. These nodes occupy the expected right para-aortic and aortocaval regions and are in close proximity to the primary mass drainage basin. No dominant left-sided para-aortic nodes above the size threshold. No celiac or mesenteric nodal enlargement.

Aorta and Major Vessels: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. The celiac, superior mesenteric, and inferior mesenteric arteries are patent. No vascular anomaly.

PELVIS:

Bladder: The bladder is moderately filled with smooth walls and no intraluminal filling defects. No pelvic sidewall masses.

Prostate: The prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic hyperplasia given the patient's age. No discrete nodule or extracapsular extension evident on CT.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No enlarged pelvic lymph nodes. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Musculoskeletal: The imaged osseous structures, including the lumbar spine, pelvis, proximal femora, and ribs, demonstrate no discrete lytic or blastic lesions to suggest skeletal metastases at this time. Multilevel degenerative disc disease is noted in the lumbar spine without significant canal stenosis on this evaluation.

IMPRESSION:

1. Very large, heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.1 cm, arising from the upper and mid poles of the right kidney, with extensive central necrosis and marked internal heterogeneity. The imaging characteristics are most consistent with a clear cell renal cell carcinoma given the hypervascular peripheral enhancement pattern and degree of necrosis. Sarcomatoid or higher-grade features cannot be excluded given the bulk of necrosis and aggressive size.

2. The right renal vein is expanded and filled with heterogeneous soft tissue, consistent with tumor thrombus extending to the renal vein-IVC junction. Definitive assessment of inferior vena cava involvement is limited on this CT; dedicated abdominal MRI is recommended prior to surgical planning to precisely characterize the cephalad extent of venous thrombus.

3. Prominent retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, most conspicuous in the right para-aortic region (approximately 2.8 cm) and aortocaval region (approximately 1.9 cm), consistent with regional nodal metastatic involvement in the setting of the primary renal malignancy.

4. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, the largest 1.4 cm in the right lower lobe, with additional nodules in the left lower lobe (0.8 cm) and right upper lobe (0.6 cm). These are most consistent with pulmonary metastases in this clinical context. No intrathoracic lymphadenopathy.

5. No hepatic lesions, adrenal metastases, peritoneal implants, or skeletal metastatic disease identified on this examination.

6. Right adrenal gland appears grossly separate from the mass on coronal reformats; MRI will better delineate this relationship.

Overall findings are consistent with advanced right renal cell carcinoma with regional retroperitoneal nodal metastases and bilateral pulmonary metastatic disease. Findings communicated to referring clinician at time of report completion.
